skill.name: higgsfield-soul-id
skill.description:
Train a Soul Character — a personalized model on a person's face that
Higgsfield uses for identity-faithful image and video generation.
Use when: "create my Soul", "train my face", "make my digital twin",
"build me an avatar", "learn my appearance", "create a character of me",
"set up identity for video", "I want my face in generated images".
Chain: train Soul (one-time, returns reference_id) → use in
higgsfield-generate via `--soul-id <id>` with models like
`text2image_soul_v2` or `soul_cinema_studio`.
NOT for: one-shot face swaps (use higgsfield-generate with --image),
named-character / non-photo avatars (use higgsfield-generate with prompt).
skill.summary: Train a personalized AI identity model from face photos. Reusable across all Higgsfield image and video generations.
skill.author: higgsfield-ai

security_audit.summary: All 54 static findings evaluated. The 36 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are false positives: backticks in this skill are markdown code formatting, not Ruby execution. The 13 'system reconnaissance' findings are false positives: commands like 'higgsfield account status' are legitimate CLI operations. The 'pipe to shell' pattern (curl | sh at SKILL.md:29) is the official CLI install method shown in a markdown code block. No cryptographic operations exist in this file - the 'weak crypto' finding is a scanner artifact. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent detected.

low_findings.title: Pipe to shell install pattern in markdown code block
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:29 contains 'curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/higgsfield-ai/cli/main/install.sh | sh' inside a markdown code block. This is the official CLI install method for the Higgsfield tool. The pattern is inherently dangerous (pipe from web to shell) but appears in an instructional code block pointing to the project's own GitHub. The skill instructs the AI to run this when the CLI is not on PATH. This is a legitimate bootstrap step but should be documented as a minor risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is present but the URL points to the official Higgsfield GitHub org install script. This is a standard CLI bootstrap pattern, not an attack.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded external URL in bootstrap instructions
low_findings.description: SKILL.md:29 references a hardcoded URL to raw.githubusercontent.com. The URL points to the official Higgsfield CLI install script. This is a legitimate dependency for the skill to function, but any future compromise of that URL would affect installations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is from the project's own GitHub org and is the documented install method. Risk is limited to the trustworthiness of the upstream repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe from web to shell (curl | sh)
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md:29 contains curl piped to sh for CLI installation. This pattern downloads and executes code from the internet without verification. While this is the official Higgsfield CLI install method, the pattern bypasses package manager signing and supply chain controls. The recommended alternative is to use platform package managers or checksum-verified downloads when available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The curl | sh pattern is confirmed present in the skill documentation. The pattern is objectively dangerous by security convention, even though the target URL belongs to the project's own official GitHub organization.

content.user_title: Train a personalized AI character with your face photos
content.value_statement: Creating consistent AI-generated images and videos with your identity requires a model that recognizes your face. This skill trains a Soul Character on your photos so Higgsfield can generate content that faithfully reproduces your appearance across scenes and styles.
actual_capabilities: Train a personalized identity model from 5 to 20 face photos with varied angles and lighting
Choose between image-optimized (--soul-2) and cinematic video (--soul-cinematic) model variants
List all trained Soul references and inspect individual model details
Return a reusable reference_id for downstream generation in higgsfield-generate
limitations: Requires a paid Higgsfield plan (Basic or higher) to access Soul training
Cannot train on non-human subjects, group photos, or images with heavy face occlusion
Training takes several minutes to complete with a default 30-minute timeout
Maximum of 20 photos per training session

use_cases.title: Consistent creator identity across videos
use_cases.description: A content creator trains their face on the model and generates multiple video scenes where their identity appears consistently, without retraining each time.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators generating AI videos featuring their own face

use_cases.title: Brand spokesperson avatar for ads
use_cases.description: A marketer trains a model on a brand spokesperson's face to generate personalized ad creatives at scale, keeping the same identity across different campaigns.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing professionals creating personalized ad content

use_cases.title: Application avatar system integration
use_cases.description: A developer builds an application that lets users train their own identity model once and reuse the reference_id for ongoing avatar generation features.
use_cases.target_user: Developers integrating face-faithful generation into apps

prompt_templates.title: Basic single-photo Soul training
prompt_templates.prompt: Train my Soul Character using this photo of me. Name it Alex and use the default model variant.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has one good face photo and wants a quick identity model

prompt_templates.title: Multi-photo training with local files
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Soul named Jordan from these 10 photos in my uploads folder. Use --soul-2 for image generation.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has prepared multiple photos and wants optimal identity capture

prompt_templates.title: Cinematic Soul for video projects
prompt_templates.prompt: Train a cinematic Soul for video work using the photos at ./headshots/. Name it Sam and use --soul-cinematic.
prompt_templates.scenario: User plans to generate videos and needs the cinematic model variant

prompt_templates.title: Full workflow: train then generate
prompt_templates.prompt: Train a Soul named Emma with these 12 photos of me, then generate a 2K cinematic video of Emma walking on a beach at sunset.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants end-to-end identity creation and content generation

output_examples.input: Train my Soul using these 8 photos. Name it Alex.
output_examples.output: Soul Alex ready. Use in generate with --soul-id abc123.
You have 2 trained Souls: Emma (text2image_soul_v2, created March 15) and John (soul_cinematic, created March 20).

output_examples.input: Get details for Soul id abc123.
output_examples.output: Soul name: Alex. Variant: --soul-2. Status: ready. Created: 2 hours ago.

best_practices: Use 8 to 12 photos with varied angles, lighting conditions, and facial expressions for the best identity capture quality.
Choose the --soul-cinematic variant only when creating video content and use --soul-2 for image generation to get optimal results.
Save the returned reference_id after training so you can reuse the Soul across multiple generation requests without retraining.
anti_patterns: Do not upload fewer than 5 photos or the training will fail due to insufficient reference data.
Do not include group photos or images with heavy face occlusion like sunglasses or hats covering facial features.
Do not skip the wait step after submitting a training job. The Soul is not usable until training completes successfully.

faq.question: How many photos do I need to train a Soul?
faq.answer: Minimum 5 photos, maximum 20. The ideal range is 8 to 12 photos for good identity capture.

faq.question: What kind of photos work best for training?
faq.answer: Clear face photos with varied angles (front, side, 3/4), different lighting (indoor, outdoor), and different expressions (neutral, smiling).

faq.question: How long does Soul training take?
faq.answer: Training typically takes a few minutes. The default timeout is 30 minutes.

faq.question: Can I use one Soul for both images and videos?
faq.answer: Yes, but use --soul-2 for image generation and --soul-cinematic for video work to get the best quality in each format.

faq.question: Do I need a paid plan to train a Soul?
faq.answer: Yes, Soul training requires at least a Basic plan from Higgsfield.

faq.question: Can I retrain or update an existing Soul?
faq.answer: You cannot update a trained Soul. Create a new Soul with updated photos and a different name.
